etiquette.title: Language
etiquette.text: English is Nigeria's official language and works everywhere for business and services - Nigerian Pidgin dominates casual street conversation, but you won't need it to get by.

etiquette.title: Greetings
etiquette.text: A handshake is standard. Greeting elders or officials first, and with visible respect, matters a lot here - it's noticed when skipped.

etiquette.title: Dress
etiquette.text: Smart-casual in Victoria Island/Ikoyi business settings; dress modestly when visiting markets or religious sites.

etiquette.title: Time
etiquette.text: "Nigerian time" is real - social plans commonly start later than stated. Business meetings are usually punctual though.

etiquette.title: Tipping
etiquette.text: Not mandatory, but rounding up for good service (drivers, waitstaff) is appreciated and increasingly expected in upscale spots.

neighborhoods.name: Victoria Island
neighborhoods.description: The business and diplomatic core - embassies, international offices, better lighting and security presence. Most visitor-friendly area.

neighborhoods.name: Ikoyi
neighborhoods.description: Upscale, residential, leafy. Good restaurants, quieter than VI at night.

neighborhoods.name: Lekki Phase 1
neighborhoods.description: Where a lot of Lagos nightlife and newer restaurants have moved. Busy, younger crowd, more built up every year.

neighborhoods.name: Yaba
neighborhoods.description: Mainland tech and university hub ("Yabacon Valley") - startups, real local food, far cheaper than the islands.

dailyRhythm.label: Traffic
dailyRhythm.text: "Go-slow" (traffic jams) is a defining feature of Lagos life, especially crossing between the mainland and the islands 07:00-09:30 and 16:30-19:30. Plan around it.

dailyRhythm.label: Business hours
dailyRhythm.text: Most offices run roughly 09:00-17:00 weekdays. Markets often open earlier and run later.

dailyRhythm.label: Weekends
dailyRhythm.text: Sundays are quieter in business districts - many people are at church for a large part of the morning.

gettingAround.label: Best app
gettingAround.text: Bolt and Uber are both widely used and are the safest, most predictable way to get around - fares for a short trip commonly start around NGN 1,000.

gettingAround.label: Avoid
gettingAround.text: Avoid unmarked/unauthorized taxis, especially getting into a car that already has other unknown passengers ("one-chance" vehicles are linked to robberies).

gettingAround.label: Danfo/BRT
gettingAround.text: Yellow danfo minibuses and the BRT bus system are what most Lagosians actually use day to day - cheap, but crowded and not the easiest first experience for a visitor.

gettingAround.label: Where to stay for ease
gettingAround.text: Victoria Island, Ikoyi, or Lekki Phase 1 keep you closer to restaurants, offices, and better transport options.

emergency.label: National emergency line (police, fire, medical, disaster)

scams.title: "One-chance" taxis
scams.description: Unmarked vehicles with people posing as fellow passengers who rob (and sometimes assault) whoever gets in. Only use Bolt/Uber or clearly marked, pre-arranged transport.

scams.title: Overly friendly unsolicited helpers
scams.description: Someone who insists on "helping" you find something, carry bags, or navigate will often expect a tip afterward, sometimes an aggressive one - politely decline unsolicited help upfront if you don't want the obligation.

scams.title: Foreigner pricing
scams.description: Prices in informal markets can inflate noticeably for visitors - agree a price before taking a good or service where none is posted.
